children.text: So, anyway. My father, he grew up in the San Felipe area, which at that time, in San Felipe, Del Rio, that was pretty isolated. You know- pura raza- you know, all around there. So, they had the white school and they had the Mexican school. And actually, the Mexican were for the blacks, too. You know, the very few blacks that lived there, but there were some. He graduated from there. As a matter of fact, one of his high school teachers ended up being my professor at Our Lady of the Lake when I first went there. Which was incredible! Because he know me instantly. I do not know how he knew me, but he says "I will never forget, you look...." I look like my father they say, so. And the last name, you know, it was not very common.

No, no, no. Este Señor Rodriguez. I’ll never forget. No actually his name was not Rodriguez (laughter) Cern. His name was Cerna. C-E-R-N-A. He was my father’s Spanish teacher and he was my Spanish teacher. Wasn’t that incredible?
Can you tell me a little bit about the neighborhood in which you grew up? What was the west side like?

Where I grew up, supposedly, we were supposed to be the better-off people. If you compare it to areas, all our surroundings, because it was a relatively new subdivision, my father bought the house on the VA loan, you know his VA loan. But the reason that we were- we were two blocks away from Culebra Street. Because at that time they did not sell to Mexicanos across Culebra. That was the exclusive Anglo neighborhood. So, they had this big wide avenue, Culebra, you know, cutting, dividing that. But that was like, right almost on the margin, right? That was a new neighborhood. We didn’t know we weren’t poor. We were poor but we didn’t know it. Everybody was the same. I guess we were. We were poor.
What was- do you remember like... there are some areas in neighborhoods that are like a central area where people congregate?

Oh, man. I wrote a short story about that. There was a monte right behind us, which we cleared. The kids! Now, we did this. We cleared it up as best as we could and we used to use it like for a baseball field, soccer, dodge ball, you name it and that’s what we use to use. You know? And, that’s where we kind of congregated – it was people from Lobrano, Blue Ridge, and Menchaca. Those three streets. I don't remember too many kids from Laurel St, except I do remember later on in life when I was a teenager, I remember Willie Velsquez and George Velasquez. They would come and see their grandmother. They lived on Laurel. But their grandmother was one of our neighbors, you know on Lombrano. That’s how I got to know them. They went to Little Flower and we were going to Christ the King, so we were kind of like you know, a little bit of rivals. Willie was older than I was, you know, but I still remember him. He use to call me "Escuincle." You know I just remember that. You know, "Escuincle." He was a, he was a- To me he was like a pachuco! You know? But I use to look up to him. You know, it was just like, wow.
I understand he was very charismatic?

He was a very kind, gentle person. And I don’t think anybody really knew him like that. But when he asked for something we always gave it. We started the "La Sociadad de la Unión," that voter registration project that turned into something really big. I remember we even use to hunt for dimes to make phone calls. You know, go to voter registration. All of this was done, you know, we weren't getting paid for it or anything. I don't know who loaned Willie the hall, but they loaned it to him and he- I think it was one of his relatives- I'm not sure. But we use to work out of that- Socidad de La Unión- and I was there with him, George, Andrea, myself. Gosh! And probably a few others but I don’t remember.
We will get to that because it is something I definitely want to talk about.

A "Pachuco" is a term that emerged in the 1930s and originally refered to a Mexican-origin boy or young man who is involved in a gang. It was usually meant as discriminatory by older generations to refer to "trouble-makers." However, young people claimed the term as part of a counter-culture "Pachuquismo" during the Chicano Movement. It was a term of pride for rejecting the dominant American culture and claiming their Mexican American one. It was expressed with fashion like the "Zoot Suit" (a big, loose, colorful suit), music, and the use of "caló" slang. The Zoot Suit had influence from African American fashion, but the "Pachuco" was distinctly Chicano.

"La Sociedad de la Unión" was a Mexican American mutual-aid society, or "mutualista." Mutualistas provided a number of services to Mexican Americans as they faced discrimination, including legal aid, financial aid, and cultural activities. The were most common from the late 1800s through the 1930s, when most began to die off due to the Great Depression and repatriation of Mexicans and Mexican Americans to Mexico. La Sociedad de la Union was founded in 1885 in San Antonio, and became the largest Mutualista in the city. It was also one of the few to survive the economic turmoil of the 1930s.
